question.text: Herbert Simon, who received the Nobel Prize in 1978, introduced the concept of Bounded Rationality. Which of the following BEST describes his idea of "satisficing"?

answer.text: Making the most optimal decision by analyzing all available information
Relying purely on intuition to make investment decisions
Delegating all financial decisions to an algorithm
Choosing an option that is satisfactory and sufficient rather than optimal, due to cognitive limitations

explanation.text: **The Logic:** "Satisficing" is a combination of "satisfactory" and "sufficient." Herbert Simon argued that humans have cognitive limitations and cannot process all available information, so they settle for a good-enough solution rather than the optimal one. Bounded Rationality falls BETWEEN full rationality and pure intuition. **The Trap:** "Analyzing all available information" describes the Standard Finance ideal of a fully rational agent, not bounded rationality. Pure intuition is also incorrect — satisficing is a middle ground. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series X-A covers rational investor assumptions under portfolio theory. Understanding bounded rationality explains why real investors deviate from these assumptions. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Recognize that your clients are satisficers — they won't read a 50-page research report. Present 2-3 well-curated options rather than overwhelming them with choices.
